- Consider whether the landing page warrants its own build pipeline entry separate from the viewer build, as its zero coupling makes it fully portable and independently deployable.
- The single inbound edge from web→web-landing is the only cross-zone edge touching this zone and is a build-time reference, not a runtime import — the landing page is fully portable and has no runtime coupling to the application
- The landing page has exactly one inbound cross-zone reference (web→web-landing: 1) which is a build-time bundler entry, not a runtime import. This makes it the cleanest boundary in the entire web package — a true static island with no observable runtime coupling.
- landing.ts is classified as [utility] rather than [entrypoint] — for a landing page the expected archetype is [entrypoint] (a one-time initialization script). A [utility] classification implies the file exports reusable functions rather than executing top-level setup, which would mean those exports are potentially consumable by viewer code without going through a declared import contract, undermining the landing page's documented zero-coupling isolation.
- landing.ts carries a [utility] archetype (expected [entrypoint] for a landing page bootstrap). If it exports reusable functions, those exports are architecturally invisible to the import graph since no consumer currently imports them — but the archetype mismatch is a signal to verify the file is not accumulating logic that belongs in web-shared or the viewer layer.
- The zone contains three distinct file formats — HTML (index.html), CSS (landing.css), and TypeScript (landing.ts) — making it the only production zone in the codebase that mixes non-TypeScript source with TypeScript source. TypeScript quality tooling (typecheck, import linting, unit test coverage) covers only landing.ts; index.html and landing.css are entirely outside automated quality gates. A rename or deletion of a CSS class referenced in landing.ts would pass all quality checks silently.
- landing.css and index.html are outside all automated quality gates (typecheck, import linting, coverage). The CSS class names and HTML structure are the primary risk surface for landing page regressions. Add explicit content assertions in build-output-contract.test.ts for at minimum the CSS class names landing.ts depends on, so that markup or style changes are detected before deployment.
